text: The surgeon initiates a laparoscopic hysterectomy, preparing the surgical field for the procedure.
The surgeon begins a laparoscopic hysterectomy, preparing the surgical field for minimally invasive removal of the uterus.
The da Vinci Surgical System is utilized for the laparoscopic hysterectomy, showcasing robotic technology in the surgical procedure.
The da Vinci Surgical System is visible, showcasing its robotic arms and instruments used for laparoscopic surgery.
Two robotic instruments are visible inside the patient's abdominal cavity, positioned for the laparoscopic hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon operates with two instruments inside the patient's abdomen. In the left hand, a grasper equipped with bipolar electrical energy is used for cautery.
The surgeon holds a grasper in the left hand equipped with bipolar electrical energy, used for manipulating and coagulating tissue during the laparoscopic hysterectomy.
The surgeon uses a bipolar electrical energy grasper for cautery during the laparoscopic hysterectomy, employing the da Vinci Surgical System.
The surgeon holds small, fine scissors in the right hand, visible within the laparoscopic view during the robotic-assisted hysterectomy using the da Vinci Surgical System.
The surgeon holds small, fine monopolar scissors in the right hand, which are used to cut and cauterize tissue during the laparoscopic hysterectomy using the da Vinci Surgical System.
The surgeon uses monopolar scissors, visible in the surgical field, as part of the robotic-assisted laparoscopic hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses the bipolar grasper to cauterize tissue within the surgical field, applying electrical energy to achieve hemostasis.
The surgeon has insufflated the abdomen with carbon dioxide gas, creating a visible space between the abdominal organs and the surgical instruments.
The surgeon has insufflated the abdomen with carbon dioxide gas, creating a visible space between the organs and instruments for laparoscopic access.
The surgeon opens the retroperitoneum and begins skeletonizing the ovarian vessels using fine scissors, revealing the vessels within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses the da Vinci Surgical System to open the retroperitoneum and skeletonize the ovarian vessels, revealing the anatomical structures within the laparoscopic view.
The surgeon points out the ovarian vessels, visible as a pedicle, which provide blood supply to the ovary and fallopian tube.
The surgeon points out the ovarian vessels, visible as a pedicle, which supply blood to the ovary and fallopian tube.
At the beginning of the hysterectomy procedure, the surgeon cauterizes and divides the ovarian vessels, which are visible as pedicles supplying blood to the ovary and fallopian tube.
The surgeon uses bipolar electrical energy with a grasper in the left hand to cauterize and divide the ovarian vessels, which are visible as a pedicle supplying blood to the ovary and fallopian tube.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to cauterize the ovarian vessels, which have been skeletonized and exposed within the retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to cauterize the ovarian vessels, taking a few moments to achieve effective control and minimize bleeding.
The surgeon continues to gain control over the ovarian blood vessels, ensuring they are securely cauterized within the retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon continues to skeletonize the ovarian vessels within the opened retroperitoneum, ensuring minimal bleeding during the process.
A third surgical instrument is visible alongside the bipolar cautery tool, assisting in the manipulation and control of the ovarian vessels during the hysterectomy procedure.
An assistant surgeon uses a third instrument to hold the uterus in place, maintaining an optimal angle for the primary surgeon's access.
The assistant surgeon holds the uterus in an elevated position, maintaining an optimal angle for the primary surgeon to access the surgical field without obstruction.
The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to cut across the ovarian vessels, with visible cauterization to minimize bleeding.
The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to cut across the ovarian vessels, demonstrating efficient and precise instrument movement within the surgical field.
The surgical instruments move efficiently and precisely as the surgeon continues the procedure, demonstrating controlled manipulation within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins dissecting the bladder flap, carefully separating the bladder from the uterus using precise instrument movements.
The surgeon dissects the bladder flap, carefully separating the bladder from the cervix and upper part of the vagina to keep it out of the surgical field during the hysterectomy.
The bladder is visually positioned on top of the cervix and the upper part of the vagina, showing the anatomical relationship between these structures during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully retracts the bladder away from the surgical field to facilitate the completion of the hysterectomy.
The surgeon carefully dissects the bladder flap, moving it out of the way to facilitate the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins skeletonizing the uterine vessels, carefully isolating them from surrounding tissues to prepare for ligation.
The surgeon begins skeletonizing the uterine vessels, revealing the blood supply to the uterus that originates from the lateral sides and converges towards the center.
The surgeon identifies the blood supply to the uterus, which is visually observed coming from the lateral sides and converging towards the center of the uterine structure.
The surgeon begins to skeletonize the uterine vessels, preparing them for subsequent coagulation and division.
The surgeon begins to skeletonize the uterine vessels, preparing them for subsequent coagulation and division.
The surgeon moves to the opposite side of the surgical field, with the assistant adjusting their position to facilitate the procedure.

The surgeon moves to the opposite side of the surgical field, demonstrating the use of a wristed robotic instrument, while the assistant uses a conventional laparoscopic instrument without wrist articulation, resulting in more awkward movements.

The surgeon divides the round ligament using robotic instruments, which have six degrees of freedom, in contrast to the assistant's conventional laparoscopic instruments with three degrees of freedom.
The surgeon divides the round ligament using surgical instruments, visibly separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon controls bleeding from a small blood vessel running through the ligament using a surgical instrument.

The surgeon controls bleeding from a small blood vessel running through the ligament using precise techniques. The camera provides a magnified view, allowing close observation of the tissue being operated on.
The surgical field remains clear as smoke generated during the procedure is efficiently evacuated through a specialized vent system.
The surgeon opens up the space on one side to begin skeletonizing the ovarian vessels, while smoke generated during the procedure is evacuated through a special vent to maintain a clear view.
The surgeon opens the space on one side to begin skeletonizing the ovarian vessels, carefully exposing these structures for further dissection.

The surgeon creates a small window on the patient's left side to maneuver instruments, ensuring the ovarian vessels are skeletonized while carefully avoiding the colon, which is slightly obstructing the view.
The surgeon creates a small window in the tissue to pass instruments through, facilitating the skeletonization of the ovarian vessels.
Both ovaries and fallopian tubes have been dissected free. The surgeon begins creating a bladder flap on the left side.

Both ovaries and fallopian tubes are dissected free, and the surgeon begins working on the bladder flap on the left side, carefully dissecting it to avoid injury to the bladder during the hysterectomy. The ureters, visible as tubes connecting the kidneys to the bladder, are also noted as important structures to avoid.
The surgeon carefully dissects the bladder flap on the left side, ensuring it is moved out of the way to prevent injury to the bladder during the hysterectomy.
The video shows the anatomical proximity of the ureters to the cervix within the female pelvis, highlighting the need for careful dissection during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully dissects the ureters away from the cervix in the female pelvis to minimize the risk of injury when cauterizing the blood supply.
The surgeon carefully dissects the ureters away from surrounding tissues to prevent injury during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully dissects and mobilizes the bladder away from the uterus, ensuring clear separation from surrounding structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects the bladder down, with the assistant occasionally re-grasping due to limited movement. Behind the uterus, the rectum is visible with a layer of fat, and efforts are made to keep it safe during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the rectum located behind the uterus, visible with a layer of fat on its surface.
The surgeon skeletonizes the uterine vessels on the patient's left side, coagulates them, and proceeds to divide the vessels using surgical instruments.
The surgeon skeletonizes the uterine vessels on the patient's left side, coagulates them, and proceeds to divide them using robotic technology, a standard step in hysterectomy procedures.
The surgeon ensures that the blood vessels on the patient's left side are securely controlled and cauterized, confirming hemostasis before proceeding to the next step of the hysterectomy.
The surgeon ensures that the blood vessels are securely controlled and cauterized, emphasizing the precision required in this robotic-assisted hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to divide the uterine vessels on the patient's right side, effectively controlling the blood supply to the uterus.

The surgeon divides the uterine vessels on the patient's right side, effectively controlling the blood supply to the uterus. Following this, the focus will shift to dissecting the cervix free, nearing the completion of the operation.
The surgeon begins dissecting the cervix free from surrounding tissues, marking the final steps of the hysterectomy procedure.
A member of the surgical team inserts a vaginal probe into the vagina, with visible movement occurring in the lower part of the surgical field.
A member of the surgical team introduces a vaginal probe into the surgical field, which is used to manipulate and position the vaginal tissues during the hysterectomy procedure.
The vaginal probe is visible within the vaginal canal, positioned to assist in the hysterectomy procedure.
The vaginal probe is used to distend the vagina and displace the bladder away from the cervix, facilitating a safer cervical dissection while protecting the bladder and ureters from potential injury.

The vaginal probe is used to distend the vagina and separate the bladder from the cervix, allowing for safer cervical dissection while keeping the bladder and ureters protected. The uterine vessels are visible and controlled.
The surgeon has successfully isolated the uterine vessels, which are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins cleaning the bladder flap, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues to ensure clear visibility and access during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses precise control to clean the bladder flap, with the vaginal probe visibly distending the surrounding tissues. The remaining blood supply to the cervix is being carefully controlled.
The surgeon uses a probe to distend the vagina and bladder away from the cervix, visibly aiding in the cervical dissection by keeping the bladder and ureters out of harm's way.
The surgeon begins to control the remaining blood supply to the cervix, using surgical instruments to manage the visible vessels.
The surgeon uses scissors equipped with monopolar energy to perform precise cutting and coagulation of tissue.
The surgeon uses monopolar energy scissors to skeletonize the junction between the vagina and cervix, carefully defining and separating the two tissues.
The surgeon uses a surgical instrument to skeletonize the junction between the vagina and cervix, carefully separating the surrounding tissue to expose these anatomical structures.
The surgeon carefully defines the area where the vagina meets the cervix and separates the two tissues using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon begins to separate the cervix from the top of the vagina, visibly creating a distinct plane between these anatomical structures.

The surgeon uses scissors to separate the cervix from the top of the vagina, with a white plastic-coated probe visible in the vagina. A surgical team member introduces a grasper through the vagina, with the patient's legs slightly separated, to retrieve the specimen.
The surgeon uses scissors to cut across the top of the vagina, continuing the separation from the cervix.
The surgeon is in the final stages of separating the specimen, completing the dissection of the cervix from the top of the vagina.
A white plastic-coated probe is visible within the vaginal cavity.
The cervix is now completely separated from the top of the vagina, completing the dissection process.
A member of the surgical team introduces a grasper through the vaginal opening, preparing to manipulate or retrieve tissue.
The patient's legs are slightly separated to facilitate access to the surgical site during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a grasper to secure the specimen, positioning it between the grasper's opening, and extracts the specimen through the vaginal canal.
The uterus is extracted through the vagina, bypassing the small incisions. The fallopian tube is visible during the process, and the specimen is successfully removed for pathological examination.
The surgeon uses a grasper to guide the uterus specimen out through the vaginal canal, with the patient's legs slightly separated to facilitate the procedure.
The fallopian tube is visible in the surgical field before being removed.
The uterus specimen is visibly removed from the surgical field, ready to be sent to the pathologist for examination.

The surgeon exchanges the right-handed instrument for a needle driver, which is used to hold a suture. The surgical tech assists by bringing in the suture, and the surgeon begins to close the top of the vagina using the needle driver.
The uterus is visibly extracted through the vaginal canal, emerging smoothly from the surgical field.
The surgeon introduces a needle driver into the surgical field, replacing the previous instrument.
The surgical technician introduces a suture into the surgical field, preparing for closure or further tissue manipulation.
The surgeon holds a needle driver in their right hand, preparing to perform suturing or manipulation tasks in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a suture to close the top of the vagina, employing precise needle placement and tension to secure the tissue.
The surgeon uses a suture to close the vaginal cuff, completing the closure of the top of the vagina.
The surgeon uses a self-locking suture device to close the vaginal cuff, demonstrating the secure and precise closure technique.
The surgeon uses a self-locking suture device to close the vaginal cuff, demonstrating its locking mechanism during the suturing process.
The surgeon threads the suture through a loop, creating the first knot that serves as a stopping point in the closure process.

The surgeon uses a suture to close the vaginal cuff, threading it through a loop to create the first knot. The suturing technique involves a wrist-turn motion, similar to opening a doorknob, which is facilitated by the wristed instruments of the da Vinci system, allowing precise and intuitive needle driving through the tissue.
The surgeon continues suturing the entire vaginal cuff, using a technique that ensures secure and precise closure.
The surgeon demonstrates precise control over the needle placement using a needle driver to efficiently close the vaginal cuff, completing the suturing process within minutes.
The surgeon completes the closure of the surgical site, ensuring the incision is securely sutured.
The surgeon approximates the uterosacral ligaments to provide support and structure to the pelvic floor, enhancing stability following the hysterectomy.

The surgeon brings the uterosacral ligaments together with a final stitch to support the pelvic floor. The robotic instruments are undocked, and the needle is retrieved from the patient's abdomen using scissors. The incisions are closed, marking the completion of the operation.
The surgeon places the final stitch to complete the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon places the final stitch in the surgical site before undocking the robotic surgical system.
The surgeon retrieves the needle from the patient's abdomen using scissors, followed by closing the incisions to complete the procedure.
The surgical instruments are removed from the patient's abdomen, indicating the completion of the hysterectomy procedure.
